summary: Feature Request: Smart Link Suggestions for Internal Note Linking

description.text:
### Checklist

- [x] Zettlr does not possess this feature (if it does, but the feature doesn't work out for you, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [x] Zettlr's current features cannot be used to approximate this behaviour (otherwise, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [x] I have searched the documentation to make sure I did not overlook it.
- [ ] I have searched both open and closed issues to see if someone has already proposed this.

### Feature Proposal

As the user types or reviews a note, Zettlr would suggest other notes that are relevant—based on similar keywords, titles, tags, or even contextual similarity—allowing the user to quickly insert links ([[Note Title]] or Markdown-style) to existing notes.

### Example Workflow

- Note Creation:

I begin writing a new note titled [[Cognitive Load Theory]] and start typing content like:

“Cognitive load can be divided into intrinsic, extraneous, and germane types. Instructional design aims to reduce extraneous load.”

- Smart Link Suggestions Triggered:

As I pause typing or hit a shortcut (e.g., Ctrl+Shift+K), Zettlr scans the text and suggests related existing notes like:

[[Instructional Design Principles]]

[[Working Memory and Learning]]

[[Cognitive Psychology Overview]]

- Linking with Ease:

I review the list in a sidebar panel or pop-up and click [[Instructional Design Principles]] to insert the link where appropriate.
Hovering over a suggestion briefly shows a snippet, helping me decide if it’s the right note.

- Further Exploration:

I follow the suggestions to re-discover related content I’d forgotten I had written. This allows me to:

Expand or refine ideas in the current note.

- Reduce duplication.

Strengthen the note network with bidirectional links.

- Later Review / Processing:

When reviewing older notes, I can use the same feature to find unlinked mentions or suggestions to connect them to newer ideas—enhancing my Zettelkasten or research network

### Caveats

- Cognitive Overload

Showing too many suggestions in real-time could clutter the interface and distract from focused writing.

Solution: Allow users to enable/disable auto-suggestions and instead trigger them manually (e.g., via hotkey).

- Unwanted or Irrelevant Suggestions

Not all suggested links will be meaningful, and irrelevant suggestions might irritate users.

Solution: Provide a way to dismiss or “ignore” suggestions and allow fine-tuning (e.g., exclude certain folders or tags).

- Privacy Concerns with AI/Context Matching

If advanced semantic suggestions use AI, users may worry about content being processed outside their devices.

Solution: Keep all processing local, or provide an opt-in for cloud-based intelligence with transparency.



### Do you wish to attempt implementing this yourself?

Yes

### Platforms

- [x] Windows
- [x] macOS
- [ ] Linux

### Additional Information

Sidebar Panel
A collapsible panel on the right side (like the current file explorer) that shows “Suggested Links” based on current note content. Clicking an item inserts a [[link]].

Hover previews to show the first few lines of a suggested note before inserting.

Option to tag suggestions as “Not relevant” to improve future suggestions.

summary: Feature Request: Goal Tracker for Word Count Progress

description.text:
### Checklist

- [x] Zettlr does not possess this feature (if it does, but the feature doesn't work out for you, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [x] Zettlr's current features cannot be used to approximate this behaviour (otherwise, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [x] I have searched the documentation to make sure I did not overlook it.
- [x] I have searched both open and closed issues to see if someone has already proposed this.

### Feature Proposal

Introduce a Goal Tracker feature in Zettlr that allows users to set and monitor word count goals — per session, per document, or per project — with live progress feedback. This feature would significantly boost productivity for writers, researchers, and students who rely on consistent output.

### Example Workflow

Set Goal:

- The user opens Zettlr to begin a writing session.
- In the toolbar or status bar, they click a new “Set Goal” button.
- They select “Session Goal” and enter a target of 1000 words.

Write and Track:

- As the user writes, the word count is tracked in real-time.
- A progress bar appears in the status bar or a small floating panel:

Visual Feedback:

- The progress bar updates live.
- When the user reaches their goal, the bar changes color or displays a small celebration animation (e.g., checkmark or confetti).
- Optionally, a subtle sound or toast notification confirms completion.

Reset or Continue:

- The next time the app is launched, the session goal resets.
- Users can also configure document-level goals that persist over time (stored in front matter or config).


### Caveats

- Zettlr already shows word counts per document — adding another word count system could be confusing if not clearly distinguished.

- Session-based tracking may behave inconsistently across OSes if session state isn’t saved 

- Adding progress bars or goal widgets might make the interface feel more cluttered, especially for minimalist users.



### Do you wish to attempt implementing this yourself?

Yes

### Platforms

- [x] Windows
- [x] macOS
- [ ] Linux

### Additional Information

1:: Set Goal Button (Toolbar / Status Bar)
Adds a small goal icon in the toolbar or status bar

2:: Progress Bar (Status Bar Widget or Floating Panel)
Minimalist status bar version

3:: Settings Panel Options
Enable/Disable Goal Tracker

Choose display style: Compact (status bar) or Detailed (floating widget)

Auto-reset session goals on app restart

summary: New File Always Defaults to .md Instead of Selected Format (e.g., JSON)"

description.text: ### Description 1. file -new-json 2. note the file name associated default to .md not json When creating a new file in Zettlr, the expected behavior is that the file extension should match the selected format (e.g., JSON). However, regardless of the chosen format, the new file is always created with a .md extension. This forces users to manually rename the file or change the extension, impacting workflow efficiency. ### Reproducing 1.Open Zettlr. 2.Click on "New File" or use the shortcut to create a new file. 3.Select a format such as .json or .yml. 4.Observe that the file is created with a .md extension instead of the selected format. ### Zettlr version 3.4.3 ### Installation Method From the Website or GitHub ### Installation Method (Other) _No response_ ### Your Platform - [ ] Windows - [x] macOS - [ ] Linux ### Architecture - [] Intel 64bit - [x] ARM 64bit (includes Apple Silicon) ### Operating System Version 15.3.1 ### Additional Information The new file should be created with the selected extension (e.g., .json or .yml) instead of defaulting to .md.

summary: Persisting Test Data: File Persists Even After Clearing Cache and Restarting in Developer Mode

description.text: ### Description 1. yarn start (application started) 2. created a new file 3. quit the session 4. restarting again (document still there which is expected) 5. quit the session 6. yarn start --clear cache (all files should be removed) 7. files still persisted The expected behaviour is that clearing the cache should remove any temporary test files, but the file remains, indicating that it is not being properly reset. ### Reproducing Steps to Reproduce: 1.Run the application in developer mode. 2.Create a new file during testing. 3.Quit the application completely. 4.Clear the cache 5.Restart the application in developer mode. 6.Observe that the previously created file is still present. ### Zettlr version 3.4.3 ### Installation Method From the Website or GitHub ### Installation Method (Other) _No response_ ### Your Platform - [ ] Windows - [x] macOS - [ ] Linux ### Architecture - [ ] Intel 64bit - [x] ARM 64bit (includes Apple Silicon) ### Operating System Version Mac os ### Additional Information A new test session should start with a fresh state(after clearing cache), and any files created in a previous session should not persist unless explicitly saved.

Closes #4039
Closes #5675
Closes #5676
Closes #5677
Closes #5678
Closes #5679
Closes #5680
Closes #5681

## Description

This adds a new _Transform_ submenu to the editor which has numerous text transforms in it.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9d6d397a-29f4-4865-88e9-933996c0a37f

## Pending Work

I have lumped the text transforms into the context menu without that much thought to ordering them.

We may want to put some thought into:

* what transforms are present in the context menu
* the order those transforms appear in the context menu
* adding the transforms to the `Edit` menu too

## Additional information

Tested on: Windows 11 Home.

> Note that I have not updated the CHANGELOG per the PR template guideline 'cos I don't know when this'll be merged, if ever 🤷

### Checklist

- [x] Zettlr does not possess this feature (if it does, but the feature doesn't work out for you, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [ ] Zettlr's current features cannot be used to approximate this behaviour (otherwise, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [ ] I have searched the documentation to make sure I did not overlook it.
- [ ] I have searched both open and closed issues to see if someone has already proposed this.

### Feature Proposal

I love the recent addition of panels in Zettlr, which allow me to view documents in stacked or side-by-side orientations. It's a great addition and really useful for consulting notes as I write, comparing different versions of documents, or even as a split-pane view for accessing different portions of the same document at once.

Occasionally, though, there are notes — to-do lists, for instance — that are good to be able to refer back to, but which don't warrant all the screen real estate of a whole split panel. I think it would nice to use a portion of the right-hand side panel as a place to open a document like this as a "sticky note."

Here's what it would look like, in my imagination:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/720e4b37-7409-4f81-8158-e70869a3057b)

Users could open a note there by dragging a tab or file manager entry over the the right-hand sidebar, similar to the way they currently drag a document to the editor's edge to create a new pane.

The sticky note could be given limited real estate in the side panel, so as not to interfere with the existing displays (references, TOC, etc.). And its boundary could be demarcated with a gray line, similar to the one that separates the "Files" section from the "Workspaces" section in the left-hand sidebar.

### Example Workflow

See above. The user drags a tab or document over the right-hand sidebar, with the result that the file opens in a small square there. The sticky note space otherwise behaves just as the document would in the editor pane. Users could close it by whatever UI method seems most reasonable.

### Caveats

None that I'm aware of.

### Do you wish to attempt implementing this yourself?

No

### Platforms

- [x] Windows
- [x] macOS
- [x] Linux

### Additional Information

_No response_

summary: RevealJS presentations export without CSS style

description.text:
### Description

Hi,

The RevealJS export seems to be broken. The slideshow is exported in HTML, but without any theme and CSS styling.

The issue seems to be related to pandoc that adds a double slash in the CSS link via cdn. See here : https://github.com/jgm/pandoc/issues/8749

Here are the broken CSS links in my export: 
```
<style type="text/css">Not found: /reveal.js@4.6.1//dist/reset.css</style>
<style type="text/css">Not found: /reveal.js@4.6.1//dist/reveal.css</style>
```

### Reproducing

Export the presentation in the Revealjs format through Zettlr's export menu.

### Zettlr version

3.4.3

### Installation Method

From the Website or GitHub

### Installation Method (Other)

_No response_

- [ ] Linux

summary: First open welcome and beginner friendly feature walk through

description.text:
### Checklist

- [x] Zettlr does not possess this feature (if it does, but the feature doesn't work out for you, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [x] Zettlr's current features cannot be used to approximate this behaviour (otherwise, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [x] I have searched the documentation to make sure I did not overlook it.
- [x] I have searched both open and closed issues to see if someone has already proposed this.

### Feature Proposal

When the application is first opened a brief tutorial walk through of the most simple workflow would greatly enhance the ease of use for new users and the adopt-ability of this software.

### Example Workflow

Maybe this could include arrows with a walk through on creating a new file. And inserting a dummy reference using the @ symbol for instance. The ability to skip should also be present. The program to a new user is very difficult to understand without reading the guide. 

### Caveats

For users more experienced returning it could be annoying. However the ability to skip even on first open will mitigate this. The extent can be very basic, however just learning the biggest feature of the app will benifit this open source project greatly.

### Do you wish to attempt implementing this yourself?

Yes

### Platforms

- [x] Windows
- [x] macOS
- [x] Linux

### Additional Information

_No response_

### Description

I just installed Zettlr Version 3.4.3 on a new Ubuntu 24.04 and I am unable to open RMarkdown (.rmd) files. I found the old Issue from 2019/20? requesting the feature and according to the zettlr-documentation, it should be possible. Unfortunately, there is no button or switch in the advanced settings menu to activate rmd-handling. 
Currently, rmd-files are not shown in the file browser on the left. If I'm trying to open rmd-files via the Ubuntu context menu, nothing happens. If I'm renaming the files from .rmd to .md, everything works fine.
Do you happen to know a quick workaround that works until a proper fix? Thank you in advance!

### Reproducing

Install zettlr 3.4.3 in ubuntu 24.04, try to open a Rmarkdown-file

3.4.3
